item.title: Black Swan(s)
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Black Swans

2021, the second year of my undergraduate degree at the University of Exeter. My house is at the very end of the highstreet, almost overlooking the River Exe. If you follow the water, you will reach the quay, bracketed with craft stalls built into the landscape, restaurants and bridges and benches that in their reliable architecture hold (what felt at the time) my entire life and identity. I have recently been gifted my first DSLR, an upgrade from a bridge camera I dutifully and loyally kept safe since I was thirteen years old. I walk up and down the quay every day, often twice a day, itching for company in the tense post-lockdown months, resigned to obsessive work at my rickety wooden desk and these brief, beautiful snatches of sun in the early morning.

Black swans (cygnus atratus) are rare in the UK. In the Merlin app (my trusty ornithology buddy), it is a solid red circle (rare) and sighting one in any given area of the UK is classified as “unlikely”. Black swans are native to southeast and southwest Australia, where they gravitate to both fresh and saltwater lakes, swamps, rivers and wetlands. They are nomadic, with varying migration patterns. In terms of appearance, they are large, black-feathered birds with white flight feathers and vibrant red bills, a bright splash that would draw any wandering eye on an afternoon stroll. Across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, they were introduced as ornamental birds in New Zealand, western Europe and Japan, with limited numbers in the US. Aside from Australia, however, their most prominent geographical association is in Dawlish, Devon. In fact, they are an established emblem of the town [https://ladysmile.co.uk/blogs/black-swans-of-dawlish-the-history-and-influence]. As such, on one routine stroll down the River Exe, I was met with a mating pair (we all deserve a holiday, right?).

After a mad dash back to my house to collect my camera, I spent at least an hour as a desperate paparazzi. Now, they remain the rarest birds (or, I would say, animal in general) that I have witnessed in the wild. I look at these pictures, and I am met not only with the beauty and majesty of their great, streamlined bodies, but a more baseline affection for both Devon and those years of mine in Exeter. I spent a lot of time photographing birds, attracted, I think, by their apparent freedom of movement. But introduction of non-native species within human-led currents is hardly freedom of movement, is it? I stood on the bank of the river and watched these birds and felt a great and affectionate calling. “We are both a little far from home, are we not?”

I had never seen black swans before, and I have not seen them since. I left Exeter and I rarely look back. However, sometimes I am looking through the thousands of photos I have taken over my life and I find these again, and for a moment I can see the crowd of them, the bright flash of red and the distinctive white stripe, and I am back there on the bank, crouching down, close enough to photograph but not to risk provoking them. Two animals facing each other off. One wild and one tame.

item.title: Stave Churches in Norway
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Stave Churches in Norway

In January of 2024, I travelled to Norway to stay at my friend’s cabin, and ski for the first time. We met while she was studying abroad in London and have since taken turns to show each other some of the dear and wonderful parts of our respective countries. We stayed a few hours from Oslo, and drove back in the chilling winter snow, my toes perpetually numb, every moment outside marked by the feeling of frost coating the inside of my nostrils. We saw great stone trolls, sweeping snowy roads, dense fir forests, and through the blanket of white, the unique, awesome spires of Norway’s surviving stave churches. Only 28 stave churches remain standing in Norway, constructed from wood, largely built between 1150 and 1350. They are common in high valleys and forest land, where the areas are less populated. While hundreds of stave churches were originally built, the majority have been destroyed through redesign, conversion, dismantlement or natural disasters like fires, storms or avalanches.

We couldn’t stay outside for long. A true Brit, even my warmest clothes were unequipped for the Norway cold. However, these great and beautiful buildings stick in my mind. I am always fond of a spire, tied as I am to the welcoming beacon of home that is the spire of Salisbury cathedral, the rich maroon against the stark white snow, the peaking headstones, the elegant and neat wood. On the same drive, we saw a moose. I have never been to Vermont, but I listen to Noah Kahan every time I set food in Norway, and when I get home, I’m always Homesick for a country that’s not my own.

item.title: Elan Valley
item.dc:creator: Sophia Jaffer
item.description:
Elan Valley

In March of 2025, I visited Wales for a few nights with one of my closest friends. We had discovered a dream BnB a year before while attending the Hay Festival at Hay-on-Wye: a love letter to the world’s unapologetic book nerds. Both employed, rolling in our minimally disposable cash, we planned a trip of beautiful breakfasts, gentle strolls through the countryside and a few tasteful hours lying in bed watching every Night at the Museum. The highlight of our trip however, and almost entirely unexpected, was the six hours we got totally lost while “hiking” the stunning Elan Valley.

We started with a reasonable stroll through an established path, where I was stuck with boundless enthusiasm to the “record” function of the Merlin app. Itching for a little more adventure, we went off path, encouraged by passers-by that the loop lasted only an hour or two, and that we were, seemingly at every point “nearly there”. We had no water, no snacks (travesty!) and no signal, so for the following six hours, we were wandering about in the hope that we were travelling somewhat in the right direction of the car park. I do have to confess that my survival skills are minimal (something that I plan to rectify in the coming years) and there was one stretch of A-road with no signal that almost sent me spiralling. We met gaggles of watchful rams and a group of four boys who led us through (our Hobbit savours), a red kite and some of the most beautiful sights I have seen in Britain. I remember often the farm we passed that carried such a malevolent energy that the crazed barking still visits me in my sleep. I often have dreams that involve farms and fearful feeling. One, which I must have had when I was fifteen or younger, was simply a soundtrack of squealing pigs being slaughtered. Anyway, I digress.

Back to civilisation, we got a great curry and slathered our blisters in antiseptic. Elan Valley remains a very fond memory, for the spontaneous “hike”, the hilarious antics, and even the inconsistent but imposing sense of dread. I would certainly go back at the earliest opportunity. I would even get lost again. Some better shoes and a packet of biscuits, then send me on my way.
